First notice the words and examples the text repeats: POST-MAURYAN TRENDS IN, INDIAN ART AND ARCHITECTURE, AN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 28, POST-MAURYAN TRENDS IN INDIAN ART AND ARCHITECTURE 29. full_notes:
Post-Mauryan Trends in Indian Art and Architecture is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that fROM the second century BCE onwards, various rulers established their control over the vast Mauryan Empire: the Shungas, Kanvas, Kushanas and Guptas in the north and parts of central India; the Satvahanas, Ikshavakus, Abhiras, Vakataks in souther n and wester n India.

The text explains that incidentally, the period of the second century BCE also marked the rise of the main Brahmanical sects such as the Vaishnavas and the Shaivas.

The text explains that there are numerous sites dating back to the second century BCE in India.

The text explains that some of the prominent examples of the finest sculpture are found at Vidisha, Bharhut (Madhya Pradesh), Bodhgaya (Bihar), Jaggayyapeta (Andhra Pradesh), Mathura (Uttar Pradesh), Khandagiri-Udaigiri (Odisha), Bhaja near Pune and Pavani near Nagpur (Maharashtra).

The text explains that bharhut Bharhut sculptures are tall like the images of Yaksha and Yakhshini in the Mauryan period, modelling of the sculptural volume is in low relief maintaining linearity.

The text explains that in the relief panels depicting narratives, illusion of three-dimensionality is shown with tilted perspective.

The text explains that clarity in the narrative is enhanced by selecting main events.

The text explains that at Bharhut, narrative panels are shown with fewer characters but as the time progresses, apart from the main character in the story, others also start appearing in the picture space.

The text explains that at times more than one event at one geographical place is clubbed in the picture space or only a single main event is depicted in the pictorial space.

The text explains that availability of the space is utilised to the maximum by the sculptors.

The text explains that folded hands in the narratives as well as single figures of the Yakhshas and Yakshinis are shown flat clinging to the chest.

The text explains that but in some cases, especially in later times, the hands are shown with the natural projection against the chest.

The text explains that such examples show how artisans who were working at a collective level had to POST-MAURYAN TRENDS IN INDIAN ART AND ARCHITECTURE Yakshini, Bharhut 4

The text explains that aN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 28 understand the method of carving.

The text explains that initially, dressing the surface of stone slabs appears as the main concern.

The text explains that later the human body and other forms were sculpted.

The text explains that the historical narratives such as the siege of Kushinara, Buddha’s visit to Kapilavastu, visit of Ashoka to the Ramgrama Stupa are carved with considerable details.

The text explains that the Buddha image at Mathura is modelled on the lines of earlier Yaksha images whereas in Gandhara it has Hellenistic features.

The text explains that one can visit museums at Mathura, Sarnath, Varanasi, New Delhi, Chennai, Amaravati, etc. to study the features of early sculptures.

The text explains that the domical stupa structure is covered with relief stupa sculptural slabs which is a unique feature.

The text explains that interior space in the composition is created by different postures of the figures such as semi-back, back, profile, frontal, semi-frontal, side, etc.

The text explains that guntapalle is a rock-cut cave site near Eluru.

The text explains that the other important site where rock-cut stupas have been excavated is Anakapalle near Vishakhapatanam.

The text explains that aN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 34 Construction of a large number of stupas does not mean that there were no structured temples or viharas or chaityas.

The text explains that we do get evidences but no structured chaitya or vihara survived.

The text explains that among the important structured viharas, mention may be made of the Sanchi apsidal chaitya structure, i.e., temple 18, which is a simple shrine temple having front pillars and a hall at the back.

The text explains that similar structured temples at Guntapalle are also worth mentioning.

The text explains that in Karla, the biggest rock-cut chaitya hall was excavated.

The text explains that though the cave’s interior was not fully finished, it shows how the carving progressed from time to time.

The text explains that many of the early vihara caves are carved with interior decorative motifs like chaitya arches and the vedica designs over the cell doors of the cave.

The text explains that 26 is very big and the entire interior hall is carved with a variety of Buddha images, the biggest one being the Mahaparinibbana image.

The text explains that cave excavation and painting were simultaneous processes and dating of the paintings follows the date of the cave excavations.

The text explains that naturalistic postures and unexaggerated facial features are used as exceptional types.

The text explains that the subsequent development of the painting tradition has been discussed in the next chapter.

The text explains that a rock-cut temple has been carved out of a single rock, a unique achievement of the artisans, which will be discussed in the next chapter.

The text explains that these rock-cut cave monuments are not natural but carved over a period of time during the ancient India mostly during the Satvahana period.

The text explains that the Bagh caves, like those at Ajanta, were excavated by master craftsmen on perpendicular sandstone rock face of a hill across the seasonal stream of Baghani.

The text explains that pOST-MAURYAN TRENDS IN INDIAN ART AND ARCHITECTURE 43 The tradition of rock-cut caves continued in the Deccan and they are found not only in Maharashtra but also in Karnataka, mainly at Badami and Aiholi, executed under the patronage of the Chalukyas; in Andhra Pradesh in the area of Vijayawada; and in Tamil Nadu, mainly at Mahabalipuram, under the patronage of the Pallavas.

The text explains that the post-sixth-century development of art history in the country depended more on political patronage than the collective public patronage of the early historic periods.

The text explains that they are toys, religious figurines as well as figurines made for healing purposes as part of the belief systems.

The text explains that the caves have been excavated in the hills along with the structured monasteries.

The text explains that perhaps it is among the very unique sites where the structured stupas, viharas and the caves are Entrance to Elephanta Caves

The text explains that apart from Guntapalle, the other important cave site is Rampaerrampallam which has very moderate small excavations but there are rock-cut stupas on the hillock.

The text explains that at Anakapalli near Vishakhapatnam, caves were excavated and a huge rock-cut stupa was carved out of the hillock during the fourth–fifth centuries CE.

The text explains that it is a unique site as it has the biggest rock-cut stupas in the country.

The text explains that many votive rock-cut stupas all around the hillock have also been excavated.

The text explains that the rock-cut cave tradition also existed in Odisha.

The text explains that describe the physical and aesthetic features of Sanchi Stupa-I.

The text explains that originally the stupa was a small brick structure which expanded over a period and was covered with stone, vedika and the torana (gateways).

The text explains that toranas are constructed in all four directions.

The text explains that the Buddha is seated in padmasana (cross-folded legs) and the right hand is in the abhayamudra, raised a little above the shoulder level whereas the left hand is placed on the left thigh.

The text explains that flexibility replaces the earlier rigidity in the images giving them a more earthy look.

Source: NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art, Chapter 4: Post-Mauryan Trends in Indian Art and Architecture

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Post-Mauryan Trends in Indian Art and Architecture, which statement is supported by the source text about AN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 30?
options: But gradually, such visual appearance was modified by making images with deep carvings, pronounced volume and a very naturalistic representation of human and animal...
Sculptures at Bharhut, Bodhgaya, Sanchi Stupa-2, and Jagayyapetta are good examples.
Narrative reliefs at Bharhut show how artisans used the pictorial language very effectively to communicate stories.
In one such narrative, showing Queen Mayadevi’s (mother of Siddhartha Gautam) dream, a descending elephant is shown.
